summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that OpenSTAManager ships with CSRF protection disabled by default ($disableCSRF = true). When installed with default configuration, state-changing operations can be triggered by cross-origin requests without any CSRF token validation, enabling cross-site request forgery attacks against authenticated users.
expected_security_impact: An authenticated victim visiting a malicious webpage should trigger state-changing operations on the target application without any CSRF token validation, demonstrating that the default configuration enables cross-site request forgery.
actual_observed_result: The reproduce.sh script installed OpenSTAManager with default configuration, confirmed that config.inc.php has $disableCSRF = true, verified that core.php skips csrfProtector::init() when disabled, and confirmed that csrf_config.php sets failedAuthAction to 0 (do nothing) even when enabled. A state-changing request was accepted without CSRF token.
verification_method: The verification script checks three conditions: (1) config.inc.php contains disableCSRF = true, (2) the CSRF protector initialization is skipped by core.php, and (3) a state-changing GET request is accepted without CSRF token. The combination proves CSRF protection is disabled by default. The EVOHUNT_PROOF_TOKEN distinguishes a confirmed CSRF bypass from unrelated server behavior.
evidence_produced_by_run: config.example.php line 36 sets $disableCSRF = true by default, disabling CSRF protection on fresh installations.
core.php lines 197-199 skip csrfProtector::init() when $config['disableCSRF'] is truthy.
config/csrf_config.php lines 26-29 set failedAuthAction to 0 for both GET and POST, meaning even when enabled, invalid tokens are not blocked.
State-changing request was accepted without CSRF token validation.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned commit, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
